my_thoughts: The correction works: not all (great deal is formatory), non-formatory = when thinking about serious things (contextual). The student asked categorical question (seeking categorical definition), which shows they understand: all vs. non-formatory (binary). They'll likely accept the correction. The episode is complete: incapacity → protocol → causal → reversal → categorical → correction. Pattern complete.
accumulated_insight: Three-turn pattern: T1: Student admitted incapacity (vulnerable helplessness)—practical protocol: think to best ability, compare results. T2: Student asked causal question (seeking correct sequence)—reversal: thinking first, perception = automatic. T3: Student asked categorical question ('Is all thinking formatory, except when we are trying to self-remember?')—seeking categorical definition via proposes binary distinction and seeks categorical rule. They understand: all vs. non-formatory (binary). The comparison_contrast mechanism corrects: not all (great deal is formatory), non-formatory = when thinking about serious things (contextual) ('No, not all, but a great deal of our thinking is formatory. But when we think about serious things, such as the ideas of this system, we either cannot think at all or our thinking is not formatory'). Pattern complete: incapacity admission → practical protocol → causal question → reversal → categorical question → correction. They've shifted: formatory = most thinking, non-formatory = serious things (contextual).
visible_response:
No, not all, but a great deal of our thinking is formatory.
But when we think about
serious things, such as the ideas of this system, we either cannot think at all or our
thinking is not formatory.
Formatory thinking is always poor, but for some problems it
is ridiculous.
